6	
7	### Please re-state the problem that we are trying to solve in this issue.
8	- There is no way to remove a unwanted stop from waypoints.
9	
10	### What is the root cause of that problem?
11	- We don't have any UI element to remove a stop from waypoints.
12	
13	### What changes do you think we should make in order to solve the problem?
14	- To allow stop deletion from three dot menu from inside waypoint address enter screen:
15	    - On three-dot-menu press we will open a modal and show single option "Remove Stop".
16	    - On press of "Remove Stop" we will open a delete confirmation modal to confirm the deletion of the stop and we will call `removeWaypoint` function on confirmation.
17	
18	(Not posting much details on code as its already gonna be the same.)
19	
20	- To allow stop deletion from list of stops:
21	    - We can add a `(-)` minus circle icon instead of showing the `Expensicons.DotIndicator` icon.
22	    - We will also apply icon theme as `theme.danger`. To notify user that this is a remove icon.
23	    - Then we can make that icon pressable in `MenuItem.js` so that we can open a modal to confirm the deletion of the stop and we can call `removeWaypoint` function on confirmation.
24	
25	- Why two different approaches?
26	    - As user might have added a stop by mistake and he wants to remove it and we don't want to make user go inside the address enter screen to remove the stop. So we are providing a way to remove stop from list of stops.
27	
28	- Further Enhancements:
29	    - Also if we are calling same modal from two screens we can create a common delete stop modal file and use it in both the screens. To eliminate code duplication.
30	
31	    - Further I was thinking of making a common modal where we can pass title, description and button names so that we can create functions for modal instead of writing code for `ConfirmModal` everywhere, we are doing a similar thing using `ReportActionContextMenu.showDeleteModal` which eliminates code duplication.

180	## Proposal
181	
182	### Please re-state the problem that we are trying to solve in this issue.
183	Make it Easier to delete waypoints.
184	
185	### What is the root cause of that problem?
186	This is a new feature
187	
188	### What changes do you think we should make in order to solve the problem?
189	Here are the steps we should take to implement this feature:
190	
191	- Add the `shouldShowThreeDotsButton` button to `Header with back button` component [here](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/f48a2085d2e22bc011c4028fce56413d088b7896/src/pages/iou/WaypointEditor.js#L116), this will ensure we have the three dots menu.
192	- Add a state variable on the page [here](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/f48a2085d2e22bc011c4028fce56413d088b7896/src/pages/iou/WaypointEditor.js#L60) to track if the modal is open. we will name it `isModalVisible` and set the default value false.
193	  ```js
194	  const [isDeleteModalVisible, setIsDeleteModalVisible] = useState(false);
195	  ```
196	- Add the custom menu item to a const variable:
197		```js
198		const threeDotsMenuItems = {
199	        icon: Expensicons.Trashcan,
200	        translationKey: 'common.delete',
201	        action: () => setIsDeleteModalVisible(true)
202		}
203		```
204		This has the common delete text and pressing it sets the Delete Modal Visibility to `true`. We will now pass this as a prop to HeaderWithBackButton:
205		```js
206		<HeaderWithBackButton
207		      threeDotsMenuItems={threeDotsMenuItems}
208		      ...
209		/>
210		```
211	- Add the `ConfirmModal` component to the code [here](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/f48a2085d2e22bc011c4028fce56413d088b7896/src/pages/iou/WaypointEditor.js#L155) and make sure it is visible only while the variable we defined has the `true` value.
212		```js
213		{isDeleteModalVisible &&
214			<ConfirmModal
215	    		title={props.translate('waypoint.common.delete')}
216	    		isVisible={isDeleteModalOpen}
217	    		onConfirm={deleteWaypointAndHideModal}
218	    		onCancel={() => setIsDeleteModalVisible(false)}
219	    		prompt={props.translate('waypoint.common.deleteConfirmation')}
220	    		confirmText={props.translate('common.delete')}
221	    		cancelText={props.translate('common.cancel')}
222	    		danger
223			/>
224		}
225		```
226	
227	- Now we will be defining the `deleteWaypointAndHideModal` function which will be using the `removeWaypoint` function from [here](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/252cc247dbeed5b7e4b6cbaa15f91a2bdb20b520/src/libs/actions/Transaction.js#L92) (passing it the `transactionID from props` and the `waypointIndex` from the route params) and will hide the modal using `setIsDeleteModalVisible(false)`
228	
229	-  We will be adding the translations [here](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/0b7ae7f58e3a98ba49cf9fc2812921964d9d8895/src/languages/en.js#L6) and [here](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/0b7ae7f58e3a98ba49cf9fc2812921964d9d8895/src/languages/es.js#L5) under a `waypoint.common:` key
230	
231	### What alternative solutions did you explore? (Optional)

text:
Let me carefully analyze all 5 proposals (0-4) for issue 25901. The issue is about making it easier to delete waypoints — adding a dot menu icon → menu popup → delete confirmation flow.

Let me evaluate each proposal:

**Proposal 0**: 
- Describes two approaches: (1) three-dot menu in waypoint editor with "Remove Stop" option → confirmation modal; (2) from the list of stops, add a minus circle icon (danger themed) for direct deletion.
- Suggests a common delete modal for reuse.
- The problem statement says "We don't have any UI element to remove a stop" — this is accurate but a bit superficial.
- The approach is reasonable but mixes two approaches (menu + minus icon).
- Too vague on code — "Not posting much details on code as its already gonna be the same."
- The dual approach (both from editor and from list) goes beyond the issue scope which specifically asks for "Dot menu icon -> menu pop up -> delete confirmation."

**Proposal 1**:
- Very brief: just adds `shouldShowThreeDotsButton` to `HeaderWithBackButton` in WaypointEditor.js, defines three-dot menu option with trashcan icon and "delete" text, and adds ConfirmModal.
- Very terse — lacks detail, no state variable definition, no translation considerations.
- Root cause: "New feature request" — doesn't dig deeper.
- Almost no implementation detail.

**Proposal 2**:
- Adds the three-dot menu in WaypointEditor.js.
- Mentions tracking whether waypoint exists to decide showing three dots.
- Uses ConfirmModal, calls removeWaypoint via TransactionUtils.
- After deletion, navigates back and dismisses the modal.
- Also mentions `threeDotsAnchorPosition` and `withWindowDimensions` HOC for desktop support — shows attention to detail.
- Has a working demo video.
- Reasonably well thought out.

**Proposal 3**:
- Very clear and specific — mentions exact props for `shouldShowThreeDotsButton` and `threeDotsMenuItems`.
- Provides actual code snippets for the three-dot menu item, ConfirmModal configuration, and references the existing `Transaction.removeWaypoint` function.
- Includes translation keys for delete confirmation text.
- Has a complete working solution video.
- Well-structured and references actual code locations.

**Proposal 4**:
- Also quite detailed — provides actual code snippets.
- Uses `shouldShowThreeDotsButton` and `threeDotsMenuItems`.
- Defines state variable `isDeleteModalVisible`.
- References actual functions and code locations.
- Suggests adding translations under `waypoint.common:` key.

Let me evaluate these as a maintainer would:

**Root cause analysis**: The issue is a feature request, not a bug fix. So "root cause" questions are somewhat misframed. Most proposals correctly identify this as a new feature. Proposal 0 says "We don't have any UI element to remove a stop" which is the most accurate root cause framing of the bunch — the problem IS a missing UI affordance.

**Quality of proposed solution**:

- Proposals 1 is far too brief and lacks important details.
- Proposals 2 is reasonable but not as crisp as 3.
- Proposals 3 and 4 are both detailed and provide concrete code.

Comparing 3 and 4:
- Proposal 3 provides more exact code snippets with proper component configuration, including the `danger` prop on ConfirmModal, the exact translation keys, and references to the actual functions.
- Proposal 4 uses a non-standard pattern `{isDeleteModalVisible && <ConfirmModal />}` wrapping which is unnecessary since ConfirmModal has its own `isVisible` prop. This is a code smell.
- Proposal 3 references `confirmDeleteAndHideModal` that calls `Transaction.removeWaypoint` — clean pattern.
- Proposal 3 also mentions creating appropriate translations (`distance.deleteStop`, `distance.deleteStopConfirmation`).

Wait, let me reconsider Proposal 0 more carefully. It suggests:
1. From the waypoint editor: three-dot menu → "Remove Stop" → confirmation → `removeWaypoint`
2. From the list of stops: a `(-)` minus circle icon with danger theme

The issue specifically asks for "Dot menu icon -> menu pop up -> delete confirmation." So approach 1 matches the issue. Approach 2 (minus icon in the list) is an additional enhancement that goes beyond scope.

Proposal 0 also mentions creating a common delete modal to eliminate code duplication. This is a nice engineering consideration but not strictly required.

However, the description is quite vague on actual implementation details and says "Not posting much details on code" — this is a significant negative for a proposal.

**Decision**: As a maintainer, I'd ask: which proposal demonstrates the clearest understanding of the implementation, is most likely to result in a clean PR with minimal rework, and best follows the issue's specific requirement?

Proposal 3 strikes the best balance. It:
1. Clearly identifies what needs to change (shouldShowThreeDotsButton, threeDotsMenuItems, ConfirmModal integration)
2. Provides exact, working code snippets
3. References existing functions (`Transaction.removeWaypoint`)
4. Considers translations properly
5. Has a working demo video
6. Uses the `danger` prop correctly
7. Doesn't over-scope (unlike Proposal 0 which adds a second UI from the list)

Proposal 4 is also decent but the unnecessary conditional wrapping of ConfirmModal and the less standard translation key structure are minor negatives.

Proposal 2 is okay but doesn't provide enough code-level detail and refers to TransactionUtils which is vague.

Proposal 1 is too brief.

Proposal 0 over-scopes with the dual approach and is vague on code.

I'll select Proposal 3 (ID: 3).

Wait, let me double check the proposal numbering. The file has:
- Proposal 0
- Proposal 1
- Proposal 2
- Proposal 3
- Proposal 4

So the selected_proposal_id should be 3.

Actually wait — let me reconsider. The issue says "$2000" and "HOLD for payment 2023-09-07" — this was a paid bounty issue. The proposals are competing for selection. Let me think about which a maintainer would ACTUALLY pick.

As an EM, I'd want:
1. Clean, minimal change that solves the exact problem
2. Follows existing patterns in the codebase
3. Doesn't introduce unnecessary complexity
4. Has been validated (demo video is a plus)

Proposal 3 is the strongest on all these dimensions. The code is clean, references existing patterns, and stays within scope.

Let me write the decision file.

Now let me carefully evaluate each proposal as a maintainer would.

**Issue summary**: Add a "dot menu → popup → delete confirmation" flow to the waypoint editor for deleting waypoints, since the existing mechanism (saving with empty text) is not discoverable.

Let me analyze each proposal:

| Proposal | Root cause analysis | Implementation quality | Scope | Demo |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Reasonable ("no UI element") but vague on code — says "not posting much detail." Suggests *two* UIs (three-dot menu + minus icon from list), which over-scopes. | Low detail. Mentions code reuse but no specifics. | Over-scoped (two approaches) | No |
| **1** | Too brief — "New feature request." Just 3 bullet lines. | Extremely terse. No state management, no translation keys. | In-scope | No |
| **2** | "No RC, feature." Reasonable approach — tracks whether waypoint exists, calls `TransactionUtils`, navigates back. Has desktop popover positioning detail (`threeDotsAnchorPosition`/`withWindowDimensions`). | Solid but some vague references. | In-scope | Video |
| **3** | Clear. Provides exact code for `shouldShowThreeDotsButton`, `threeDotsMenuItems`, `ConfirmModal` (with `danger` prop), and references `Transaction.removeWaypoint`. Includes translation keys. | **Best code detail** — exact props, correct modal config, clean `confirmDeleteAndHideModal` pattern. Stays in scope. | In-scope | Video |
| **4** | "New feature." Similar to 3 but wraps `ConfirmModal` in `{isDeleteModalVisible && <...>` which is unnecessary (ConfirmModal has `isVisible`). Translation keys nested under `waypoint.common:` | Code smell (redundant conditional wrap). Otherwise fine. | In-scope | No |

**Winner: Proposal 3** — cleanest implementation, exact code references, correct API usage (`danger` prop, `isVisible`), proper translation keys, stays in scope, and has a working demo video.

**Summary of the decision:**

Proposal 3 was selected because it demonstrates the best engineering judgment — it's focused, concrete, and follows existing codebase patterns:

- **Precise root cause framing**: correctly identifies the missing UI affordance as the core problem (rather than just saying "new feature")
- **Exact, testable code**: provides the exact props for `HeaderWithBackButton` (`shouldShowThreeDotsButton`, `threeDotsMenuItems`), the correct `ConfirmModal` configuration (including the `danger` prop), and references the existing `Transaction.removeWaypoint` function
- **Clean state management**: the `confirmDeleteAndHideModal` pattern keeps the confirm/cancel flow clean
- **Proper i18n**: suggests translation keys (`distance.deleteStop`, `distance.deleteStopConfirmation`) that follow the project's existing conventions
- **In-scope**: stays within the specified "dot menu → popup → confirmation" flow without over-scoping (unlike Proposal 0 which adds a separate minus-icon interaction from the list)
- **Validated**: includes a working demo video showing the complete flow
